reasoning: The qwen model's 'unsafe' findings are false positives: (1) FindOxide.cmake's 'PROPAGATE' syntax is a harmless CMake error, not malicious obfuscation; (2) picorv32.sh is an unused example script not executed during makepkg. The deepseek model correctly verified all build-relevant files as safe. PKGBUILD and build processes show no risks.
disagreement_analysis: qwen overreacted to non-executable files (FindOxide.cmake with invalid syntax, picorv32.sh as unused example) while deepseek focused on actual build components. Coverage differences caused unnecessary flags.
coverage_issues: deepseek missed src/nextpnr/ice40/picorv32.sh (though irrelevant to build)
false_positives: FindOxide.cmake: Invalid CMake syntax misinterpreted as malicious; actual harmless build config
picorv32.sh: Example script flagged despite no execution during makepkg
learnings: File selection: Prioritize files referenced in PKGBUILD/build functions over examples/CI
Prompt quality: Improve CMake syntax understanding to reduce false positives
False positive reduction: Whitelist non-build scripts (examples, tests) unless PKGBUILD-invoked
Coverage alignment: Cross-check high-risk file types (CMake, scripts) between models
